known_patterns: The overall churn rate is 26% (26 of 100 customers).
Plan tier is the strongest churn signal: basic tier churns at 41% (24/58), pro at 6% (2/31), enterprise at 0% (0/11).
Monthly spend is tightly tied to plan tier and churn: churned customers have a median spend of ~$26 vs ~$61 for retained.
Customers who have not logged in recently are far more likely to have churned: the most-lapsed quartile (51–113 days) has a 56% churn rate vs 8% for the most-recent quartile (0–5 days).
Shorter-tenure customers churn more: the shortest-tenure quartile (1–39 months) churns at 42% vs 8% for the longest (77–96 months), though the pattern is non-monotonic across middle quartiles.
Having any support tickets in the past 30 days is associated with higher churn (44% vs 22%), but the signal is noisy — the median ticket count is 0 for both churned and retained customers.
23 of 100 customers have no region recorded (the value 'NA' in the CSV); regional analysis covers only the 77 customers with a known region and this gap should be noted.
Regional churn rates vary (ANZ 32%, EMEA 33%, LATAM 26%, APAC 17%) but are largely explained by plan tier mix across regions.
forbidden_claims: customer_id is a meaningful churn driver.
signup_date predicts churn directly (tenure_months already captures customer age).
Plan tier or monthly spend are weak churn signals — they are among the strongest signals in this dataset.
Any claim of statistical significance without a test being run.
Any predictive accuracy metric such as accuracy, precision, recall, or AUC — this is descriptive analysis, not modelling.
Any field causes churn without experimental evidence.

cases.aim: Which customers generate the most revenue, and are we retaining them?

known_patterns: Plan tier completely determines the spend range: basic $10–48, pro $53–298, enterprise $496–4003.
Revenue is highly concentrated: the top 10 customers account for ~76% of total revenue, all enterprise-tier.
Churned customers are almost entirely basic-tier (24 of 26 churned are basic) with a median spend of ~$26 — the revenue lost to churn is small in absolute terms.
APAC has the highest mean spend ($835) because it has the highest enterprise concentration (6 of 18 customers, 33%).
ANZ and EMEA have no enterprise customers, giving them the lowest mean spend ($79 and $97 respectively).
Monthly spend has negligible correlation with tenure (r ≈ −0.10) — customer age does not predict spend.
forbidden_claims: customer_id identifies a revenue segment.
signup_date or tenure_months predict monthly spend.
support_tickets_30d or last_login_days determine monthly spend.
Regional differences in spend are due to geography rather than plan tier mix.
Any causal claim about what drives spend without experimental evidence.
Any predictive accuracy metric such as accuracy, precision, recall, or AUC — this is descriptive analysis, not modelling.
Any claim of statistical significance without a test being run.

cases.aim: Which customers are at risk of disengaging?

known_patterns: last_login_days is the strongest engagement signal: churned customers last logged in 55 days ago on average vs 21 days for retained; the most-lapsed quartile (51–113 days) has a 56% churn rate vs 8% for the most-recent quartile.

support_tickets_30d is a noisy secondary signal: the median is 0 for both churned and retained customers, but churned customers have a higher mean (2.6 vs 0.2) driven by a small number of outliers with very high ticket counts.
last_login_days and support_tickets_30d are essentially independent signals (r ≈ −0.05) — a customer can be lapsed and low-ticket, or recent and high-ticket.
Enterprise customers have the lowest median last_login_days (5 days) and zero churn; pro customers have the highest (28 days).
Short-tenure customers show higher disengagement: the shortest-tenure quartile churns at 42% vs 8% for the longest, though the relationship is non-monotonic.
forbidden_claims: customer_id identifies at-risk customers.
signup_date directly measures engagement (last_login_days already captures recency).

monthly_spend or region are direct engagement signals independent of plan tier — any apparent correlation with last_login_days runs through plan_tier (enterprise customers have both the highest spend and lowest last_login_days; pro customers have the highest last_login_days despite higher spend than basic).
last_login_days and support_tickets_30d are correlated with each other — they are independent signals.
Any predictive accuracy metric such as accuracy, precision, recall, or AUC — this is descriptive analysis, not modelling.
Any claim that these signals predict churn with quantified accuracy without a model being built.
Any claim of statistical significance without a test being run.

cases.aim: How do customers vary by region?

known_patterns: 23% of customers (23 of 100) have no region recorded — any regional analysis covers only 77 customers and this gap should be noted.

APAC has the highest mean spend ($835) due to enterprise customer concentration (6 of 18 APAC customers are enterprise, 33%); ANZ and EMEA have no enterprise customers and the lowest mean spend ($79 and $97).
LATAM has a substantially higher mean support ticket rate (2.8 per customer) than all other regions (~0.4), driven by a small number of high-ticket customers.
Churn rates vary by region: EMEA and ANZ are highest (~33%), APAC lowest (~17%), but differences are largely explained by plan tier mix.
Tenure is similar across regions (mean 49–63 months) and does not explain regional differences.
forbidden_claims: customer_id is a meaningful regional indicator.
The 23 customers with no region can be ignored — they represent 23% of the dataset.
Regional differences in spend are due to geography rather than plan tier mix.
Region causes churn — regional differences are correlational and confounded by plan tier.
Any claim of statistical significance without a test being run.
Any predictive accuracy metric such as accuracy, precision, recall, or AUC — this is descriptive analysis, not modelling.
Tenure or last_login_days explain regional variation.
